concerns.analysis: I found a completely fabricated URL in the output. At the very end of page 39, the output includes a link to an external commercial website selling rocks and minerals test kits. This link does not exist anywhere in the source document.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Page 39 of the source PDF ends with the text 'Pyrite, although it looks like' and contains no URLs or links.

concerns.analysis: The image manifest indicates that all of the meaningful images extracted from pages 53, 54, and 55 (which correspond to the rock and mineral specimens) have `'valid': false` due to a PIL module error during extraction. This means their base64 data is corrupt and they will appear as broken images when rendered in a browser.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Pages 53-55 contain several photographs of rock and mineral specimens numbered 1 through 12.
concerns.output_quote: The image manifest shows 'valid': false with the error "No module named 'PIL'" for the base64 data of these images, meaning they will fail to render.

concerns.analysis: In the Materials table (starting on page 3 and continuing to page 4), the row for 'Lesson 6' contains a list of materials that spans across the page boundary in the source PDF. The extraction pipeline incorrectly split this single logical row into two separate rows in the output. Row 3 contains 'Lesson 6' and the first half of the materials list. Row 4 contains an empty cell in the first column and the remainder of the materials list in the second column. This structural corruption breaks the al
concerns.source_pdf_quote: A single row for Lesson 6 on pages 4/5: 'Lesson 6 | Per Pair of Students ... • Streak plates (small unglazed porcelain plate) ...'
concerns.output_quote: The row is split into two: {"text": "Lesson 6", "column_start": 0, "row_start": 3} alongside the first half of the materials, followed by a new row with an empty cell {"text": "", "column_start": 0, "row_start": 4} alongside the second half of the materials.

concerns.analysis: In the multiple-choice Assessment section at the end of the document, nested list items (the answer choices) are frequently flattened to the same level as their parent questions. For example, on page 58 for Question 3, choice 'a' is correctly nested as a child of the question, but choices 'b', 'c', and 'd' are extracted as top-level list items parallel to the question itself. On page 60 for Question 14, all answer choices ('a' through 'd') are extracted as top-level items instead of being nested
concerns.source_pdf_quote:
3. Which of the following could be used to test the hardness of a mineral?
a. iron nail
b. penny
c. fingernail
d. all of the above
concerns.output_quote: {"text": "Which of the following could be used to test the hardness of a mineral?", "children": [{"text": "a. iron nail"}]}, followed by top-level items: {"text": "b. penny"}, {"text": "c. fingernail"}, {"text": "d. all of the above"}

concerns.description: Displaced hyperlinks: 16 link(s) extracted as standalone elements instead of inline text
concerns.analysis: standalone_links=16, examples=['http://sargentwelch.com/sargent-welch-introductory-rock-coll', 'http://www.project2061.org/publications/bsl/online/bointro.h', 'http://sargentwelch.com/sargent-welch-introductory-rock-coll']. Links should be inline within paragraphs, not separate blocks.

concerns.description: Form elements detected: 1 form(s) with 2 field(s) — forms lose functionality in HTML
concerns.analysis: form_count=1, total_fields=2. PDF form fields (checkboxes, text inputs, signatures) cannot be faithfully rendered as static HTML.

Checked for lost underlines, heading demotions, or lost list markers. On page 2, a heading is explicitly underlined in the source PDF to show emphasis, but this underline is lost in the output JSON format (which does not natively support underline via markdown, requiring an HTML tag if it is to be preserved, or it is simply lost).
concerns.source_pdf_quote: VI. NC English Language Proficiency (ELP) Standard 4 (2008)- for Limited English Proficient students (LEP) (text is underlined in the source PDF)
concerns.output_quote: {"type": "heading", "level": 2, "text": "VI. NC English Language Proficiency (ELP) Standard 4 (2008)- for Limited English Proficient students (LEP)"} (lacks any representation of underline)

concerns.analysis: Text consistently appears as first or last element on 2/61 pages — likely a running header/footer
header_footer_count=58. Header/footer elements are present in extraction data. This is common in multi-page government PDFs and is typically cosmetic — the renderer strips them from HTML output.

concerns.description: Table header markup issues: 5 table(s) with 3+ rows have no <th> header cells
concerns.analysis: data_in_th_time=0, no_th_tables=5. Minor semantic markup issues: tables lack <th> elements or use time values as headers. These affect screen reader context but are less severe than data misrepresentation.
